README.md:
# Custom Store MCP Shopping Server — REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION

**This is a reference implementation, not a runnable server. You must
implement the store adapter (see `src/store.ts`) against your store's own
product and cart layer before it will work.**

Generated by Adeptra Merchant for **mystore.example.com**.

## What this is, and isn't

A bespoke store has no standard API Adeptra can generate a working client
for. What this scaffold gives you instead: a correct, complete, boundary-safe,
UCP-shaped MCP shopping server, with the store-specific data access left as
eight clearly-marked integration points in `src/store.ts`. `src/server.ts`
(the MCP server itself, tool registration, the payment boundary) is complete
— you should not need to touch it. Only `src/store.ts` needs work.

## The hard boundary — read this first

**This server is designed to read your catalog and build carts. It must
never take payment — checkout stays on your own store.** Every cart
response's `continue_url` comes from your adapter's `getCheckoutUrl()`; the
shopper completes the purchase there, through your existing payment setup.
Adeptra generates and configures this server — it does not host it, and it
must never touch your money.

Unlike the WooCommerce and Wix scaffolds, where the boundary is enforced by
which real API endpoints the generated code calls, **here the boundary is a
CONTRACT**: `src/types.ts`'s `StoreAdapter` interface has no payment method,
no order-creation method, no customer/admin method — that absence is the
entire boundary. This interface intentionally cannot express payment
authorization. **If you find yourself needing to add a payment or
order-creation method to this interface, stop — that is outside this
server's scope**, by design, the same as it is for every other Adeptra-
generated MCP scaffold. Nothing at the type-system level can stop you from
adding one anyway; this is a discipline the interface asks of you, not code
that structurally prevents it.

## About UCP protocol conformance — what this server is, and isn't

This server implements UCP's shopping capabilities directly, not a
simplified approximation. Three precise statements, not one blanket claim:

1. **Catalog: conformant.** `search_catalog`, `lookup_catalog`, and
   `get_product` use UCP's canonical tool names and argument shapes
   (`docs/specification/catalog/mcp.md`). Catalog's own conformance
   requirements don't include a transport requirement, so this server meets
   them fully.
2. **Cart: conformant on tool names, argument shapes, and session
   semantics — NOT on transport.** `create_cart`/`get_cart`/`update_cart`/
   `cancel_cart` use UCP's canonical names, the required `meta["ucp-agent"]`
   envelope, id-based resource addressing, and full-replacement update
   semantics exactly as `docs/specification/cart-mcp.md` defines them. But
   that same document's Conformance section states plainly: *"A conforming
   MCP transport implementation MUST ... Support HTTP transport with
   streaming."* This server uses stdio — the standard "one local process per
   agent session" MCP deployment model — not a hosted HTTP service. That
   makes it transport-non-conformant for cart, disclosed here rather than
   left to discover the hard way.
3. **Checkout: deliberately not declared, not implemented.** UCP's
   `complete_checkout` tool is defined to accept payment credentials and
   place the order — this server (and every Adeptra-generated MCP scaffold)
   intentionally never implements anything like it. Payment stays with the
   merchant: the cart's own `continue_url` field (UCP's own sanctioned
   cart-to-checkout handoff mechanism, not a bespoke addition) hands off to
   this store's own checkout page. The UCP manifest this server pairs with
   does not declare `dev.ucp.shopping.checkout` — declaring it without
   implementing `complete_checkout` would be non-conformant; simply not
   declaring it is a fully spec-sanctioned profile (capabilities are
   independently adoptable, and `docs/specification/cart.md` itself frames
   cart as "basket building without the complexity of checkout").

**What IS true regardless:** deploying this server and pointing your UCP
manifest's `dev.ucp.shopping` endpoint at it genuinely satisfies Adeptra's
own capability-declaration and endpoint-reachability checks for cart and
catalog — those checks read your manifest's JSON and confirm the endpoint
responds; they don't require literal MCP transport conformance. Checkout's
capability signal either fails honestly or, if you've attested to
deliberately using this handoff profile, reflects that choice instead of
failing — see your Adeptra dashboard.

## Implement the adapter — what, and in what order

Open `src/store.ts`. Every one of its eight methods currently throws an
`"IMPLEMENT-THIS: ..."` error explaining exactly what it must return — read
`src/types.ts` first, it's the full contract every method implements
against. Recommended order:

1. **`getProduct` and `searchProducts`** — read-only, the easiest to verify
   against your store directly (see "Test each method" below).
2. **`getCart`** — read-only; returning an empty cart (`lineItems: []`) for
   "no cart yet" is a valid, expected implementation, not a stub.
3. **`addToCart`, `updateCartItem`, `removeCartItem`, `emptyCart`** — the
   cart-mutating group; implement together since they typically share
   cart-lookup logic. `emptyCart` must be a REAL, total removal (used to
   guarantee a fresh cart on `create_cart` and a genuine cancellation on
   `cancel_cart`) — not a session flag.
4. **`getCheckoutUrl`** — last, and read its doc comment again before
   writing it: it must return a URL, and must never process payment itself.

## Test each method before running the server

You do not need a running MCP server to test `src/store.ts` — it's a plain
object of async functions. After `npm run build`:

```bash
node --input-type=module -e "
import { storeAdapter } from './dist/store.js';
console.log(await storeAdapter.getProduct('some-real-product-id'));
"
```

Swap in a real id/query for each method and confirm the shape matches
`src/types.ts` before wiring anything else up. This catches most mistakes
far faster than debugging through the full MCP server.

## What Adeptra can and can't verify here

Because this scaffold can't know your stack, **Adeptra cannot verify your
implementation works against your store.** The startup check in
`src/server.ts` only confirms the adapter has been started (none of its
methods still throw the placeholder error) — it is not a correctness check.
Testing each method yourself, as above, is the only way to know it's right.

## Prerequisites

- Node.js 18 or later, on a host you control (a VPS, a small cloud instance,
  a container — anywhere that can run a long-lived Node process). Adeptra
  never hosts this for you.
- A developer comfortable writing a small TypeScript adapter against your
  store's own product/cart data access (a REST API, a database, an SDK —
  whatever your store already has).

## Install

```bash
cd mcp-server
npm install
npm run build
```

`npm run build` succeeds even before you implement `src/store.ts` — the
stubs are valid TypeScript that compiles cleanly; they only fail at
*runtime*, deliberately (see the startup check above), not at build time.

## Configure

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Edit `.env` and set `STORE_BASE_URL` and `CHECKOUT_URL` to your real values
— then add whatever else your own `src/store.ts` implementation needs (API
keys, tokens, etc.); this scaffold reads nothing else by default.

## Run

```bash
npm start
```

Until `src/store.ts` is implemented, this prints a clear message naming
exactly which adapter methods still need work, and exits — not a stack
trace. Once implemented, this starts the MCP server over stdio, the standard
way MCP clients (agent runtimes, UCP-speaking hosts) launch and talk to a
local MCP server process.

## Connect it to your UCP manifest

Once this server is implemented, deployed, and reachable, point your UCP
manifest's `dev.ucp.shopping` service endpoint at its deployed address
(replacing the placeholder Adeptra's manifest fix left there), so agents
that discover your manifest know where to reach this shopping server. The
manifest Adeptra generates for you declares catalog + cart only — never
checkout, since this server doesn't implement it (see "About UCP protocol
conformance" above).

## Prefer not to implement this yourself?

Adeptra also offers a paid setup service that implements this adapter for
you against your store — see your Adeptra dashboard or ask your Adeptra
contact if you'd rather not do this yourself.

## You must complete

- [ ] Implement all eight methods in `src/store.ts` against your store —
      this artifact does nothing until you do.
- [ ] Set `STORE_BASE_URL` and `CHECKOUT_URL` in `mcp-server/.env` (plus
      anything else your implementation needs).
- [ ] Deploy this to a host you control (Node 18+) — Adeptra does not host it for you.
- [ ] Point your UCP manifest's shopping service endpoint at this server's
      deployed address, then re-run your Adeptra analysis — that's what turns
      "cart/catalog capability enabled once deployed" into a genuine pass.
- [ ] If your Adeptra checkout-capability signal is failing because checkout
      isn't declared, and you're intentionally using this handoff profile,
      attest to that via the checkout-handoff opt-in in your Adeptra
      dashboard so it scores correctly instead of failing.

## Tools this server exposes

| Tool | Does |
|---|---|
| `search_catalog` | Search the catalog by keyword |
| `lookup_catalog` | Look up one or more products by id |
| `get_product` | Get one product's full details by id |
| `create_cart` | Start a new cart session (always empty) |
| `get_cart` | Get the current cart contents |
| `update_cart` | Replace the cart's full contents (add/change/remove in one call) |
| `cancel_cart` | Cancel the cart session (empties it for real) |
.env.example:
STORE_BASE_URL=https://REPLACE-WITH-YOUR-STORE-URL.example
CHECKOUT_URL=https://REPLACE-WITH-YOUR-CHECKOUT-URL.example

# This scaffold reads no other environment variables by default. Add
# whatever credentials YOUR store's API needs (API keys, tokens, session
# secrets, etc.) once you implement src/store.ts against your own store —
# this file is a starting point, not an exhaustive list.
